q: Who are the three founding peoples of Canada?
expl: The guide states that to understand what it means to be Canadian, it is important to know about our three founding peoples: Aboriginal, French and British.

q: Today, the term Aboriginal peoples refers to which three distinct groups?
expl: The guide says the term Aboriginal peoples refers to three distinct groups: Indian (First Nations), Inuit and Metis.

q: What does the word "Inuit" mean in the Inuktitut language?
expl: Inuit means "the people" in the Inuktitut language.

q: Where do the majority of Metis people live?
expl: The Metis are a distinct people of mixed Aboriginal and European ancestry, the majority of whom live in the Prairie provinces.

q: What is the name of the dialect spoken by the Metis?
expl: The Metis come from both French- and English-speaking backgrounds and speak their own dialect, Michif.

q: Which is the only officially bilingual province in Canada?
expl: The guide states that New Brunswick is the only officially bilingual province.

q: What are the two official languages of Canada?
expl: English and French are the country's official languages, and the federal government is required by law to provide services in both.

q: According to the guide, how many Anglophones (people who speak English as a first language) are there in Canada?
expl: The guide says there are 18 million Anglophones and seven million Francophones.

q: How many Francophones (people who speak French as their first language) live in Canada, according to the guide?
expl: There are seven million Francophones; the majority live in Quebec, with one million in Ontario, New Brunswick and Manitoba.

q: The Acadians are the descendants of which group?
opts: British Loyalists who fled the American Revolution
French colonists who began settling in the Maritime provinces in 1604
Irish settlers who arrived in the 1800s
Aboriginal peoples of the Atlantic coast
expl: The Acadians are the descendants of French colonists who began settling in what are now the Maritime provinces in 1604.

q: What event is known as the "Great Upheaval"?
opts: The migration of Aboriginal ancestors from Asia
The placing of Aboriginal children in residential schools
The deportation of more than two-thirds of the Acadians between 1755 and 1763
expl: Between 1755 and 1763, during the war between Britain and France, more than two-thirds of the Acadians were deported from their homeland, an ordeal known as the "Great Upheaval."

q: What did the House of Commons recognize in 2006?
opts: That New Brunswick is officially bilingual
That French is an official language
That the Quebecois form a nation within a united Canada
That the Acadians deserved an apology for the Great Upheaval
expl: The House of Commons recognized in 2006 that the Quebecois form a nation within a united Canada.

q: How were the territorial rights of Aboriginal peoples first guaranteed?
opts: Through the Royal Proclamation of 1763 by King George III
Through the British North America Act of 1867
Through a House of Commons motion in 2006
expl: Territorial rights were first guaranteed through the Royal Proclamation of 1763 by King George III, which established the basis for negotiating treaties.

q: What did the federal government do in 2008 regarding residential schools?
opts: Formally apologized to the former students
Transferred the schools to the provinces
Placed Aboriginal children in residential schools
expl: In 2008, Ottawa formally apologized to the former students of residential schools.

q: Canada is the only country in North America that is a:
expl: The guide states that Canada is the only constitutional monarchy in North America.

q: The phrase "Peace, Order and Good Government" comes from which document?
opts: The British North America Act of 1867
expl: "Peace, Order and Good Government" is a key phrase in Canada's original constitutional document of 1867, the British North America Act.

q: According to the guide, about what percentage of Aboriginal people are First Nations?
expl: About 65% of Aboriginal people are First Nations, while 30% are Metis and 4% Inuit.

q: About half of First Nations people live on reserve land in approximately how many communities?
expl: Today, about half of First Nations people live on reserve land in about 600 communities, while the other half live off-reserve, mainly in urban centres.

q: Since the 1970s, most immigrants to Canada have come from which part of the world?
expl: The guide states that since the 1970s, most immigrants have come from Asian countries.

q: What is the largest religious affiliation in Canada?
expl: The largest religious affiliation is Catholic, followed by various Protestant churches.

q: In Vancouver, what percentage of the population speaks Chinese languages at home?
expl: In Vancouver, 13% of the population speak Chinese languages at home; in Toronto, the number is 7%.

q: The basic way of life in English-speaking areas of Canada was established by hundreds of thousands of settlers from which groups?
expl: The basic way of life in English-speaking areas was established by hundreds of thousands of English, Welsh, Scottish and Irish settlers, soldiers and migrants from the 1600s to the 20th century.

q: Who was John Buchan, the 1st Baron Tweedsmuir?
opts: A popular Governor General of Canada (1935-40)
The founder of the Hudson's Bay Company
expl: John Buchan, the 1st Baron Tweedsmuir, was a popular Governor General of Canada from 1935 to 1940 who said immigrant groups "should retain their individuality and each make its contribution to the national character."

q: In becoming Canadian, newcomers are expected to embrace:
opts: Democratic principles such as the rule of law
expl: The guide states that in becoming Canadian, newcomers are expected to embrace democratic principles such as the rule of law.

q: Most Quebecers are descendants of approximately how many French settlers from the 1600s and 1700s?
expl: Most Quebecers are descendants of 8,500 French settlers from the 1600s and 1700s and maintain a unique identity, culture and language.

q: New Brunswick is the only officially bilingual province in Canada.
expl: True. The guide states that New Brunswick is the only officially bilingual province.

q: The majority of Canadians were born outside of Canada.
expl: False. The majority of Canadians were born in this country, and this has been true since the 1800s, although Canada is often referred to as a land of immigrants.

q: The Inuit live in small, scattered communities across the Arctic.
expl: True. The Inuit live in small, scattered communities across the Arctic, and their knowledge of the land, sea and wildlife enabled them to adapt to one of the harshest environments on earth.

q: The majority of Francophones in Canada live in New Brunswick.
expl: False. The majority of Francophones live in the province of Quebec; one million Francophones live in Ontario, New Brunswick and Manitoba.

q: According to the guide, gay and lesbian Canadians enjoy equal treatment under the law, including access to civil marriage.
expl: True. Canada's diversity includes gay and lesbian Canadians, who enjoy the full protection of and equal treatment under the law, including access to civil marriage.

q: Who discovered insulin, a hormone to treat diabetes?
opts: Sir Frederick Banting and Charles Best
Alexander Graham Bell and Sandford Fleming
expl: Sir Frederick Banting of Toronto and Charles Best discovered insulin, which has saved 16 million lives worldwide.

q: What was the name of Terry Fox's 1980 cross-country run to raise money for cancer research?
expl: In 1980, Terry Fox, a British Columbian who lost his right leg to cancer at age 18, began the "Marathon of Hope" to raise money for cancer research.

q: Who invented basketball in 1891?
expl: Basketball was invented by Canadian James Naismith in 1891.

q: The discovery of oil in 1947, which began Canada's modern energy industry, took place in which province?
expl: The discovery of oil in Alberta in 1947 began Canada's modern energy industry.

q: The era of rapid change that Quebec experienced in the 1960s is known as the:
expl: Quebec experienced an era of rapid change in the 1960s known as the Quiet Revolution.

q: What does the Official Languages Act of 1969 guarantee?
opts: That every province must be officially bilingual
That all Canadians must learn both English and French
French and English services in the federal government across Canada
That Quebec may separate from Canada
expl: The Official Languages Act (1969) guarantees French and English services in the federal government across Canada. It followed the Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism established in 1963.

q: Canada joined the North American Aerospace Defence Command (NORAD) together with which country?
expl: Canada joined with the United States in the North American Aerospace Defence Command (NORAD).

q: What is the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)?
opts: A free trade agreement with the United States
A military alliance of democratic countries of the West
expl: Canada joined with other democratic countries of the West to form the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), a military alliance, at the start of the Cold War.

q: In which conflict did Canada participate in a UN operation defending South Korea, with 500 dead and 1,000 wounded?
expl: Canada participated in the UN operation defending South Korea in the Korean War (1950-53), with 500 dead and 1,000 wounded.

q: When was unemployment insurance (now called "employment insurance") introduced by the federal government?
expl: Unemployment insurance was introduced by the federal government in 1940. Old Age Security was devised in 1927, and the Canada and Quebec Pension Plans in 1965.

q: Old Age Security was devised as early as what year?
expl: Old Age Security was devised as early as 1927.

q: In what year were the Canada and Quebec Pension Plans established?
expl: The Canada and Quebec Pension Plans were established in 1965.

q: Who provides publicly funded education in Canada?
expl: Publicly funded education is provided by the provinces and territories.

q: What happened to the Canadian Constitution in 1982?
opts: It was replaced by the British North America Act
It was amended without the agreement of Quebec
It was rejected in a national referendum
It was suspended during the Cold War
expl: After much negotiation, in 1982 the Constitution was amended without the agreement of Quebec.

q: In which years was Quebec sovereignty defeated in provincial referendums?
expl: The movement for Quebec sovereignty was defeated in a referendum in 1980, and sovereignty was again defeated in a second referendum in 1995.

q: The Group of Seven, founded in 1920, developed a style of painting to capture what subject?
expl: In the visual arts, Canada is historically perhaps best known for the Group of Seven, founded in 1920, who developed a style of painting to capture the rugged wilderness landscapes.

q: What did Emily Carr paint?
opts: The forests and Aboriginal artifacts of the West Coast
expl: Emily Carr painted the forests and Aboriginal artifacts of the West Coast.

q: In 1956, about 37,000 refugees who escaped Soviet tyranny were welcomed to Canada from which country?
expl: Canada welcomed thousands of refugees from Communist oppression, including about 37,000 who escaped Soviet tyranny in Hungary in 1956. Later, over 50,000 Vietnamese sought refuge in Canada after 1975.

q: Which Canadian invention is Alexander Graham Bell associated with?
expl: Alexander Graham Bell hit on the idea of the telephone at his summer house in Canada.

q: What did Joseph-Armand Bombardier invent?
expl: Joseph-Armand Bombardier invented the snowmobile, a light-weight winter vehicle.

q: What did Sir Sandford Fleming invent?
opts: The worldwide system of standard time zones
expl: Sir Sandford Fleming invented the worldwide system of standard time zones.

q: Reginald Fessenden contributed to the invention of radio by doing what?
opts: Sending the first wireless voice message in the world
expl: Reginald Fessenden contributed to the invention of radio, sending the first wireless voice message in the world.

q: What did Dr. John A. Hopps invent?
expl: Dr. John A. Hopps invented the first cardiac pacemaker, used today to save the lives of people with heart disorders.

q: The Canadarm, a robotic arm used in outer space, was invented by:
expl: SPAR Aerospace / National Research Council invented the Canadarm, a robotic arm used in outer space.

q: Mike Lazaridis and Jim Balsillie of Research in Motion (RIM) are known for which famous invention?
expl: Mike Lazaridis and Jim Balsillie of Research in Motion (RIM), a wireless communications company, are known for its most famous invention: the BlackBerry.

q: Who scored the winning goal for Canada in the 1972 Canada-Soviet Summit Series, often called "the goal heard around the world"?
expl: In 1972, Paul Henderson scored the winning goal for Canada in the Canada-Soviet Summit Series.

q: Wayne Gretzky, one of the greatest hockey players of all time, played for the Edmonton Oilers from 1979 to 1988.
expl: True. One of the greatest hockey players of all time, Wayne Gretzky, played for the Edmonton Oilers from 1979 to 1988.

q: In 1985, Rick Hansen circled the globe in a wheelchair to raise funds for cancer research.
expl: False. Rick Hansen circled the globe in a wheelchair to raise funds for spinal cord research. It was Terry Fox who ran to raise money for cancer research.

q: Canada's high standard of living is maintained by the hard work of Canadians and by trade with other nations, in particular the United States.
expl: True. Canadians enjoy one of the world's highest standards of living, maintained by hard work and by trade with other nations, in particular the United States.

q: Aboriginal people were granted the right to vote in federal elections in 1948.
expl: False. Aboriginal people were granted the vote in 1960. It was Japanese-Canadians who gained the right to vote in 1948.
